chunk_summary: Guidelines for introducing allergenic foods during complementary feeding (6–24 months) emphasize risk stratification, safe preparation, and early introduction to reduce allergy risk while ensuring choking prevention.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of early allergenic food introduction to prevent allergies.
Learn risk stratification methods for introducing peanuts based on eczema severity.
Identify safe preparation techniques for allergenic foods to prevent choking.
Recognize symptoms of allergic reactions and appropriate emergency responses.
key_facts: Introduce allergenic foods like egg, peanut, dairy, wheat, and fish starting around 6 months when developmentally ready.
Infants with severe eczema and/or egg allergy should have peanut introduced as early as 4–6 months after evaluation (e.g., allergy testing).
Infants with mild-to-moderate eczema can start peanut at home around 6 months, while those without eczema or food allergies can start freely.
Avoid whole nuts, thick nut butter, or chunks of nut due to choking risk; use smooth peanut butter thinned with water or mixed into soft foods.
Introduce allergens during daytime when the child is well, starting with small amounts and monitoring for hives, vomiting, cough, wheeze, or lethargy.
Respiratory symptoms, repeated vomiting, or collapse require emergency care and specialist follow-up.
reference_quote: "The NIAID addendum guideline recommends that infants with severe eczema and/or egg allergy have peanut introduced early (as early as 4–6 months) but often after evaluation, which may include allergy testing and supervised feeding depending on results."
document_key_topics: Complementary feeding for infants aged 6–24 months
Risk stratification for allergenic food introduction
